sections.heading: Core frame: Development, Adaptive Behavior, Context, and Learner Variability
sections.content: For Development, Adaptive Behavior, Context, and Learner Variability, Describe the student’s strengths and needs across intellectual functioning, conceptual-social-practical adaptive behavior, communication, academics, health, sensory-motor access, relationships, culture, language, interests, and environments. Presume competence and avoid using a score or label to infer understanding, preferences, placement, or potential. Apply the 5322 lens from preschool through grade 12: preserve dignity, belonging, communication access, self-determination, family partnership, cultural and linguistic responsiveness, age respect, safety, and high expectations. Connect each support to observable outcomes, implementation evidence, and current requirements.
keyTerms: Development, Adaptive Behavior, Context, and Learner Variability

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a kindergarten teacher notices that one child reaches several language milestones later than classmates. What is the most appropriate first interpretation?
choices: Development follows broad patterns, but individual variation requires information from multiple sources and settings before conclusions are drawn.
Any difference from a class average proves a disability.
The child should immediately receive a fixed disability label.
Chronological age no longer matters when planning instruction.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Milestones provide useful reference points, not stand-alone diagnoses. Educators should document functioning across domains, contexts, and time and involve qualified team members before attributing a pattern to disability. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a student solves multistep problems during small-group instruction but rarely begins the same work during a noisy whole-class period. What should the teacher examine first?
choices: Assume the student has forgotten the underlying skill.
Compare task demands, environmental conditions, supports, and performance across the two contexts.
Lower every academic expectation for the student.
Treat the difference as deliberate noncompliance.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Performance reflects an interaction among learner skills, task demands, supports, and context. Comparing conditions can identify a removable barrier before the team changes expectations or infers a stable trait. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a teacher wants to understand a learner’s self-regulation needs. Which information is most useful?
choices: A single global rating completed months ago.
The individual support profile without current observations.
Observations of triggers, strategies used, recovery, and successful conditions across routines.
The average behavior of the rest of the class.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Self-regulation is best understood through specific, current observations of what precedes difficulty, how the learner responds, which strategies help, and whether patterns differ by setting or task. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a multilingual learner has difficulty following rapid oral directions in English but demonstrates the task after a peer models it. Which interpretation is best?
choices: The performance automatically establishes an intellectual disability.
Visual modeling should be removed to reveal the true score.
Home-language information is irrelevant to educational planning.
Language access may be affecting the demonstration of knowledge, so the team should gather culturally and linguistically appropriate evidence.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Difference is not disability. The team should distinguish language acquisition and access from disability by using multiple methods, appropriate language supports, and information about performance in the learner’s languages. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a middle school student with an intellectual disability is embarrassed when an adult uses age-inappropriate materials for a functional routine. What principle should guide revision?
choices: Use age-respectful materials while matching the instructional steps and supports to the learner’s current needs.
Match all materials to developmental level regardless of age.
Stop teaching functional skills until grade-level mastery is reached.
Ask peers to complete the routine for the student.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Instruction can be individualized without sacrificing dignity. Materials and contexts should be chronological-age respectful, while prompts, task steps, and response modes are adapted to the learner’s present performance. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: For a student with an intellectual disability, a student with a specific learning disability reads grade-level text accurately but cannot organize written paragraphs. Which response is best?
choices: Target the documented area of academic need instead of assuming the disability affects every subject in the same way.
Provide identical remediation in all academic areas.
Infer low overall intelligence from the one difficulty.
Replace instruction with permanent adult completion.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Specific learning disabilities can affect particular psychological processes and academic skills unevenly. Instruction should be based on the learner’s actual pattern of strengths and needs, not a global assumption. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: For a student with an intellectual disability, a student with autism covers their ears during fire-drill announcements and then misses directions. Which support is most responsive?
choices: Interpret the response as intentional defiance.
Plan a predictable, accessible warning and communication routine while teaching the student how to use it.
Exclude the student from every future drill.
Require exposure without preparation until the response stops.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Sensory and communication needs can affect access and behavior. A predictable warning, accessible direction, and taught coping or communication response support participation while preserving safety requirements. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: For a student with an intellectual disability, a student with ADHD begins work when directions are chunked and a checklist is visible. What does this pattern suggest?
choices: The student knew the work and was choosing to fail before.
The checklist has changed the academic standard.
Executive-function supports are improving task initiation, organization, and monitoring.
The disability affects intelligence rather than self-management.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: ADHD may affect executive functions such as initiation, working memory, inhibition, and organization. External structures can support performance without changing the learning target or implying weak ability. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: For a student with an intellectual disability, a student with an emotional or behavioral disability argues mainly during unpredictable peer-group tasks. What perspective is most useful?
choices: Assume the behavior will occur equally in every setting.
Conclude that peers alone caused the disability.
Remove all social learning opportunities permanently.
Examine interactions among the learner, task, peers, expectations, and consequences rather than locating the problem only within the child.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: An ecological perspective considers how behavior emerges through interactions between the learner and the environment. Context-specific data can guide prevention, instruction, and reinforcement of more effective responses. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: For a student with an intellectual disability, which information is essential when considering educational needs associated with intellectual disability?
choices: Information about both intellectual functioning and adaptive behavior across conceptual, social, and practical domains.
A reading score by itself.
Physical appearance and medical history only.
The expectations of one classroom without other contexts.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Intellectual disability involves limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behavior. Educational planning therefore considers functional conceptual, social, and practical performance across relevant environments. knowledgeChecks.prompt: While developing a person-centered program for a student with an intellectual disability, which present-level statement best supports individualized planning?
choices: On three curriculum writing samples, the student wrote a clear topic sentence in two of three paragraphs and included supporting details in one.
The student is significantly below grade level.
The student dislikes writing and needs help.
The student has SLD in written expression.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Present levels should describe current, measurable academic or functional performance and how disability affects progress. Specific baseline evidence supports goal design and later comparison. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: While developing a person-centered program for a student with an intellectual disability, which IEP goal is measurable?
choices: The student will improve reading fluency.
Given a 150-word instructional-level passage, the student will read at least 120 correct words per minute on three consecutive weekly probes.
The student will try harder when reading aloud.
The student will become a confident reader by spring.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: A measurable annual goal states the condition, observable behavior, criterion, and often a consistency requirement. These features permit repeated progress measurement and instructional decisions. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: While developing a person-centered program for a student with an intellectual disability, an IEP contains a measurable goal but no description of how progress will be measured. What is missing?
choices: A guarantee that the student will meet the goal.
A comparison with every classmate each week.
The measurement method and schedule for periodic progress reports.
A new individual support profile for each skill deficit.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: IDEA requires the IEP to describe how progress toward annual goals will be measured and when periodic reports will be provided. A goal without a monitoring plan is incomplete for decision making. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: While developing a person-centered program for a student with an intellectual disability, a student’s goal targets independent paragraph planning, but the service description addresses only oral reading. What should the team do?
choices: Keep the mismatch because services and goals are unrelated sections.
Delete the goal rather than examine the program.
Use a generic service package assigned by category.
Align specially designed instruction, services, supports, and progress monitoring with the identified need and goal.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: IEP components should form a coherent program: present needs lead to measurable goals, services and supports address those goals, and progress measures show whether the program is effective. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

Have a peer perform all experimental decisions and actions.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: In an inclusive, safe, predictable learning environment, a class loses instructional time because arrival routines change each day. Which preventive support is best?

Wait for errors and deliver increasingly severe consequences.
Post a long rule list without instruction or practice.
Change the routine often to build flexibility immediately.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: In an inclusive, safe, predictable learning environment, a student tears worksheets after receiving a long independent assignment, and the task is then removed. Which information is most relevant to a functional hypothesis?

The antecedent task, the observable behavior, and the consequence of escaping or delaying the work.
Whether the behavior annoys adults.
A list of consequences used with other students.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: In an inclusive, safe, predictable learning environment, an FBA suggests that disruption reliably produces peer attention. Which BIP component is most important?

Remove academic instruction permanently.
Teach and reinforce an efficient appropriate way to request attention while adjusting antecedents and consequences that maintain disruption.
Teach a break request even though escape is not the identified function.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: In an inclusive, safe, predictable learning environment, which replacement behavior is best for a student who leaves work to escape confusing directions?

Ask for peer attention unrelated to the task.

Use a taught help card to request clarification or a brief guided start.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: In an inclusive, safe, predictable learning environment, a teacher says, “You opened your notebook and began within one minute—that followed our start routine.” Why is this feedback effective?

It delays feedback until the end of the month.
It depends on comparing the student with classmates.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student is studying ecosystems with classmates but cannot independently decode the grade-level article. Which support best preserves substantive participation?
choices: Provide an adapted text and read-aloud or symbol supports while retaining the core ecosystem concepts and an accessible way to demonstrate comprehension.
Assign an unrelated coloring page because the article is difficult.
Require independent decoding before allowing participation in the science discussion.
Have a paraprofessional supply every answer without presenting the text.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Access supports should reduce the print barrier without replacing the academic construct. Retaining the shared concepts and an independent response mode supports inclusion, presumes competence, and produces meaningful evidence of comprehension.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A learner recognizes grocery words on flash cards but not on aisle signs or a family shopping list. Which instruction is most likely to make the skill useful?
choices: Continue flash-card practice in the same seat until every response is immediate.
Teach the words within age-respectful shopping routines using varied lists, fonts, locations, and opportunities to communicate a choice or ask for help.
Replace all print with adult verbal directions during shopping.
Teach only one store display so the visual context never changes.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Authentic practice with varied examples connects word recognition to purposeful action and communication. Programming variation and support fading builds generalization rather than restricting performance to one memorized material set.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student is beginning to connect sounds and letters and enjoys shared stories. Which literacy plan is most appropriate?
choices: Wait for spontaneous reading because direct instruction may reduce autonomy.
Teach isolated symbols only and postpone access to stories indefinitely.
Use explicit, systematic sound-symbol instruction with cumulative review and immediate application in meaningful, age-respectful text.
Use materials designed for much younger children regardless of student interests.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Systematic teaching and cumulative practice address skill acquisition, while meaningful age-respectful text sustains purpose and access to literacy. The plan avoids assuming that current performance limits future learning.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student can select a new vocabulary symbol during drill but not during class discussion. How should vocabulary instruction change?
choices: Repeat the same picture until it is memorized and call the concept mastered.
Require speech as the only acceptable demonstration of vocabulary knowledge.
Introduce many unrelated words at once without checking meaning.
Teach the concept with multiple examples and nonexamples, connect it to familiar experiences, and elicit receptive and expressive responses through accessible modes.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Concept learning requires more than association with one picture. Multiple exemplars, explicit connections, and accessible receptive and expressive responses reveal understanding and support use across instructional contexts.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student listens to a short passage and answers accurately by selecting pictures but gives no spoken response. How should the teacher interpret the result?
choices: Accept the established accessible response mode, provide planned wait time, and verify comprehension across several questions and contexts.
Score every nonspeaking response as incorrect.
Have an adult point to the answer before the student responds.
Infer full comprehension from one correct selection and stop collecting data.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Comprehension should not be confounded with speech production or partner prompting. Accessible responding, adequate processing time, and repeated evidence provide a more valid picture of what the student understands.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student uses AAC to choose a topic but an adult composes the entire message. Which change would make writing more student directed?
choices: Offer an accessible set of generative words and phrases, let the student select and revise the message, and provide only the support needed for the targeted writing step.
Have the adult write a polished message and ask the student to approve it afterward.
Restrict the student to copying one sentence chosen by staff.
Delay all writing until the student can use a standard keyboard independently.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Writing instruction should preserve authorship, not merely produce an adult-created product. Accessible generation and revision choices reveal student intent while systematic support can be faded as competence grows.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: During shared writing, a student activates a switch only to copy a teacher-selected sentence. Which redesign best supports expression?
choices: Continue copying because original ideas are unnecessary for writing.
Elicit the student ideas through an accessible communication mode, visibly incorporate those ideas, and teach a specific composing or editing skill within the shared product.
Ask peers to decide what the student probably wants to say.
Remove the student from group writing for separate tracing practice.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Shared writing becomes substantive when the learner contributes meaning and receives explicit instruction in the composing process. Accessible authorship maintains inclusion and provides evidence beyond motor copying.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A transition class is teaching students to complete community forms. Which lesson is most age respectful and useful?
choices: Use worksheets designed for young children because the steps are simpler.
Have an adult complete every form to prevent mistakes.
Teach the relevant fields on realistic, privacy-protected forms, use the preferred access method, and fade prompts while checking independent accuracy.
Practice a signature only and ignore the other information needed for the task.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Authentic, age-respectful materials connect writing to self-determination and community participation. Privacy safeguards, accessible entry, and prompt fading build useful independence without reducing the task to imitation.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student uses a symbol for stop during personal-care instruction, but staff continue the routine because the schedule is tight. What should the educator do?
choices: Ignore the message so the student learns compliance.
Remove the symbol from the communication system.
Require spoken refusal before adults respond.
Honor the communication promptly, determine the immediate concern, and teach safe ways to negotiate, request help, or resume when appropriate.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: A reliable way to refuse, pause, or seek help is fundamental communication and self-determination. Honoring the message establishes its meaning while instruction can address safety, alternatives, and any necessary return to activity.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student has ideas for a report but cannot spell enough words to produce them conventionally. Which plan best balances access and instruction?

Withhold content writing until conventional spelling is mastered.
Accept only copied sentences because they look accurate.
Have an adult replace the student ideas with simpler content.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Expression should not be blocked by a single transcription barrier. Alternate access permits authentic composition now, while targeted explicit instruction supports continued growth in spelling, language, or motor skills.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student is planning a purchase with a fixed budget and can use a calculator but needs help deciding which items fit. What should instruction emphasize?
choices: Model a budget strategy with prices and a calculator or visual organizer, have the student compare totals and choices, and fade prompts across authentic purchases.
Remove the calculator and require mental computation before shopping.
Have an adult select every item and announce the total.
Practice entering unrelated numbers without making a decision.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: The instructional target is mathematical reasoning within a meaningful routine, not denial of an appropriate tool. Authentic comparison, accessible representation, and prompt fading build agency and generalizable budget use.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student reads digital times but misses activities when a schedule changes. Which mathematics instruction best addresses the need?
choices: Continue matching identical clock cards at a desk only.
Teach elapsed-time and schedule checking with the actual daily routine, varied times and locations, accessible cues, and practice responding to changes.
Have an adult direct every transition without showing the time relation.
Eliminate all schedule changes rather than teach flexibility.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Time use involves relating clock information to duration, sequence, and action. Varied practice in real routines plus accessible cues supports transfer and reduces dependence on adult transition commands.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A learner names coins accurately but cannot determine change during a café purchase. Which instructional sequence is strongest?
choices: Repeat coin naming without connecting it to purchase totals.
Let the cashier decide whether the change is correct every time.
Use concrete money or realistic representations, model a change-making strategy, move toward symbolic calculation, and practice with varied authentic transactions.
Require abstract written algorithms before using any concrete representation.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Concrete-representational-abstract instruction links money concepts to the actual decision the learner must make. Systematic modeling and varied transactions build accuracy without treating isolated naming as functional mastery.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student is learning to measure ingredients during a culinary routine. What is the safest and most instructionally useful approach?
choices: Use hand-over-hand assistance for the entire routine indefinitely.
Avoid all measurement and have an adult prepare every ingredient.
Teach measurement separately and allow unsupervised appliance use immediately.
Analyze the routine, explicitly teach measuring with adapted tools and visual cues, follow the individualized safety plan, and fade assistance using step-level data.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Task analysis can integrate mathematical quantity with safe participation. Accessible tools, explicit teaching, the existing safety plan, and prompt-fading data support both competence and autonomy without exceeding educator scope.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent intellectual-disabilities educator, self-advocate, family, AAC, assistive-technology, health, transition, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric validation remain pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student can count objects to ten but is participating in a grade-level unit comparing data displays. Which plan is most appropriate?
choices: Teach the needed counting and comparison explicitly while providing an accessible, substantive role in collecting, representing, and interpreting the shared data.
Replace the unit with unrelated preschool counting sheets.
Wait for complete mastery of counting before allowing any data work.
Assign the student to distribute pencils while peers analyze the data.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Foundational and grade-level instruction can occur together. An accessible role in the shared mathematical process preserves ambitious content, while explicit number teaching addresses the prerequisite needed for increasing independence.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When selecting or administering assessment for a student with an intellectual disability, a vocabulary test is used to decide whether a student can solve multistep equations. What is the primary concern?
choices: Validity, because the evidence does not adequately support the intended interpretation and use.
Reliability, only because the score is numerical.
Efficiency, because the test is short.
Norming, because every assessment must be nationally normed.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Validity concerns whether evidence supports the interpretation and use of scores. A measure dominated by a different skill cannot justify a conclusion about the intended construct. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When selecting or administering assessment for a student with an intellectual disability, a progress measure gives very different scores on consecutive days even though instruction and performance appear stable. What quality is in question?

Reliability or consistency of measurement and scoring.
The student’s eligibility category.
The length of the IEP.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When selecting or administering assessment for a student with an intellectual disability, why should an eligibility team use multiple assessment tools and strategies?

The highest score should automatically replace all others.
Different sources can test converging explanations and prevent one measure from becoming the sole criterion.
Informal information is never relevant.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When selecting or administering assessment for a student with an intellectual disability, a newly arrived multilingual learner is assessed only in rapid academic English. What is the central evaluation concern?

Assume low performance proves disability.
Exclude parent and language-history information.
Use the native language or appropriate communication mode and a format most likely to show what the learner knows, unless clearly infeasible.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Evaluation procedures must reduce racial, cultural, linguistic, sensory, manual, and communication barriers so results reflect the intended aptitude or achievement rather than an access limitation. Wait for failure without changing instruction.
Promise that intervention data alone will determine eligibility.

A parent request automatically makes the student eligible.
Only standardized tests may be used in evaluation.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When using progress, functional, or alternate-assessment evidence, what is the role of parent information in an evaluation?

flashcards.front: During interdisciplinary and family collaboration for a student with an intellectual disability, two team members disagree about whether an intervention is working. What is the strongest next step?

flashcards.front: What evidence defines an intellectual-disability support profile?

caseStudyDecisionLabs.title: Elementary inclusion and communication
caseStudyDecisionLabs.caseText: A fourth-grade student participates in science with an AAC device, visual vocabulary, peer lab roles, and scheduled communication-partner support. Recent records show strong concept sorting and limited independent initiation. The family reports that the student initiates frequently during cooking and gardening at home. Classroom observation shows that adults often anticipate needs, hold the device, or answer before adequate wait time. The IEP includes communication, grade-level science access, and peer-participation goals. The team is considering more adult assistance because work completion is slow. Review the case as an integrated record rather than treating any score, diagnosis, behavior, or staff opinion as decisive. Identify strengths and learner-family priorities, the construct and decision, missing or potentially invalid evidence, access barriers, the least intrusive instructional response, responsible team roles, and the outcome and fidelity data needed for follow-up. Preserve confidentiality, dignity, age respect, belonging, communication access, health and safety, and student agency. Distinguish accommodations from modifications and educational preparation from medical or legal decisions. Verify current requirements and individualized plans before acting. caseStudyDecisionLabs.title: Adolescent transition and agency
caseStudyDecisionLabs.caseText: A sixteen-year-old student identifies paid work, public transportation, friendships, and greater control over daily schedules as priorities. Current instruction emphasizes isolated worksheets and a simulated classroom store. Community observations show successful greeting and stocking routines when natural cues and picture sequences are available, but staff provide continuous verbal prompts. Family members support community instruction and want transparent safety planning. Vocational, communication, travel, and academic evidence comes from different staff using inconsistent definitions. caseStudyDecisionLabs.title: Early learner and family priorities
caseStudyDecisionLabs.caseText: A preschool child uses gestures, vocalizations, pictures, and emerging device selections across play and care routines. The family prioritizes communication during meals, sibling play, and neighborhood activities. The program has proposed table-based discrete practice for most of the day because standardized results are low. caseStudyDecisionLabs.title: Behavior, health, and functional communication
caseStudyDecisionLabs.caseText: A student’s refusal and self-injury occur mainly during lengthy tasks, unexpected transitions, and occasions when an AAC system is unavailable. A recent health change may affect comfort. Data use broad labels rather than observable definitions, and staff responses vary from removing all work to repeating demands rapidly. The student independently requests breaks in one therapy setting. The team must protect safety while coordinating health review, direct observation, communication teaching, antecedent supports, and a lawful individualized plan. caseStudyDecisionLabs.title: Secondary academics and community generalization
caseStudyDecisionLabs.caseText: A high-school student solves purchases with adapted materials in class but not at a grocery store, reads familiar environmental words, and uses a calculator accurately when steps are visually organized. The student wants to shop independently and manage earnings. Instruction has used one worksheet format, one adult, and fixed prompts.
